item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: replying for visibility: image has been modified from Flickr user Koshy Koshy's image under the CC Attribution 2.0 Generic License. it is a normal tiger. edits by me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pattern Screamers! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Patterns in the static

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello！It's a coincidence that I chose articles to translate into Chinses from English wiki yesterday. And all the 3 articles I chose are yours, 2659, 3904, and 2949.

It seems like you like to write animals, especially cats?

I like this article, the description about "steel wool", or, alternatively, "warm and rough" is interesting for me because it is what I imaged how the things inside TV touches feel.

Also, I like your 3904, it's wonderfully wried.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally find this a better read than 543, though I originally voted that one up. 543 at this point doesn't feel particularly ominous or out of the ordinary and could be described as some sort of visual or associative disorder. This one seems both more consistent and less… spooky?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely true! They just use a digital encoding standard now. As far as I can tell, the transmission equipment is exactly the same, just not the format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The staring-at-static-and-eventually-seeing-things-in-it bit has been done, back in Series I. Not necessarily mutually exclusive, but I thought I'd point it out, since it is very similar.

That one stuck with me (and thus this instantly brought it to mind) because I start seeing patterns in static after a few seconds, so if I stared at it for hours, I probably would start seeing crazy things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
modern TVs still have NTSC tuners though

nerd gripe aside, I like it. it doesn't kill you or shred your mind or destroy the world. it's not scary or dreadful; it just is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like, what's even going on here?

Migration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with TL333s, the phasing out of analog TVs and their widespread replacement with digital TVs as part of the containment procedures is pretty groovy, and I can dig the concept behind strange acts (like staring at static for extremely long periods of time) altering reality briefly (such as summoning a static cat.)

+1. This was a fun little article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it's an interesting idea, I'm not a fan of the lack of a story behind it. I was hoping that the 'static cat' would do more than just find a place to go and disappear.

EDIT: No vote for now. Forgot to say that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that helping phase out analog TVs is part of the containment procedures. I'm also really intrigued that these things take a lot of effort to bring into reality, and then they just go somewhere else. Like, what's even going on here? It's fascinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short, sweet and mysterious. Really good stuff.

Only one critique comes to me. Having these surprisingly detailed statistics about 2949's occurrence right at the beginning of the description seemed like an odd order to present the information. You want to start off the description with an actual description of the anomaly, right? I know the story probably paces better this way, but it still felt a bit jarring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a kid, I used to stare at the static for what seemed like hours, trying to discern patterns amidst the random noise. Sometimes it was just the faintest ghost of a signal from some unidentifiable channel, more than once I was convinced I'd stumbled on a signal from an alien world.
I was a weird kid. That kid would have loved this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it's pretty much under control, since it's extremely unlikely to occur anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice idea, but why haven't the Foundation tried to capture and interrogate or experiment on these "Static Ghosts"? There's room for experiment logs.

Also, if it's uncontainable, and we're not sure it's harmless, shouldn't it be Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about just exactly like that?

"Future appearances of SCP-2949 outside containment would require a civilian to have access to one of the few remaining affected televisions, and to purposefully observe static on said television for more than 12 hours. As this combination of events is extremely unlikely to occur, reappearance of SCP-2949 is not considered a major concern. Nevertheless, any instance of SCP-2949-1 reported in the wild… etc."

That's basically "Come on, no one's gonna stare at static for 12 hours" in formal-ese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose I enjoy the fair bit of scientific scepticism in this article, which overcome my usual apprehension towards scare quotes used in SCP articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right, the wording is pretty bad in that one. Not sure how I can word it to basically reassure the reader that "don't worry, this isn't a breach because no one is going to stare at tv static for twelve hours straight"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this! I like the way it seems like just an odd natural phenomenon, doing its own thing. It works much better this way than if it were a single anomalous tv. Also explains why digital television became so popular!

At one point you refer to "an instant" of the scp— I think the singular of "instances" is just "instance."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand this sentence: "As the appearance of SCP-2949 and SCP-2949-1 requires specific and unlikely conditions, its appearance is considered unlikely enough to be a major cause for concern, and does not constitute a widespread threat to normalcy."

I think you mean, "As the appearance of [SCP…etc] requires specific and unlikely conditions, it is not considered likely enough to be a major cause for concern, and […etc]", but as it stands, it almost reads the opposite.

Edit: I also need to know if static cats have normal meows, or if they go "kkkrrrrrrrrrrrshhhshhhhhh" all the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd imagine by some kind of statistical approximation? /shrug It's meant to be a lot, as a rare phenomenon that occurs barely under the veil of reality that could be summoned if you do something really really weird and obsessive, like stare continuously at static for six hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few things.
close

SCP-2949 is a phenomenon affecting approximately 3% of all analogue television sets ever produced

That is a lot of televisions. Also how was that figure determined?

appearance of non-humanoid faces

Can faces really even be non-humanoid? Doesn't anything face-like enough to be called a face already sort of look like a person?

vague movements in the background noise

Static already is a sort of vague movement, right?

Continuous observation of the phenomena

I think this should be the singular "phenomenon", unless I'm reading incorrectly.

Such surfaces through which SCP-2949-1 instances have been seen disappearing into include mirrors, windows, and other television screens.

I'm not sure you really need to give examples of reflective surfaces.


While I like this article, and really like the idea of the Foundation pushing for the forced-obsolescence of a medium, this feels a little lacking to me at the moment. It's just a bit too short and underdeveloped for my taste. Tentative neutral vote, though I might swing either way upon reread tomorrow morning.
